mūla.devanāgarī: सर्वधर्मान्परित्यज्य मामेकं शरणं व्रज | अहं त्वा सर्वपापेभ्यो मोक्षयिष्यामि मा शुचः
mūla.iast: sarvadharmānparityajya māmekaṃ śaraṇaṃ vraja | ahaṃ tvā sarvapāpebhyo mokṣayiṣyāmi mā śucaḥ
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 18 (Mokṣa-Sannyāsa-Yoga (The Yoga of Liberation by Renunciation)), verse 66
mūla.speaker: Krishna

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: ? न मत्तः अन्यत् अस्ति इति अवधारय इत्यर्थः
। धर्माः सन्तु न सन्तु वा किं तैरन्यसापेक्षैः। भगवदनुग्रहादेव त्वन्यनिरपेक्षादहं कृतार्थो भविष्यामीति निश्चयेन परमानन्दघ
त्वाम् एवं निश्चितबुद्धिं सर्वपापेभ्यः सर्वधर्माधर्मबन्धनरूपेभ्यः मोक्षयिष्यामि स्वात्मभावप्रकाशीकरणेन
सर्वपापेभ्यो मोक्षयिष्यामि मा शुचः इत्येतदन्तं वच उवाचेत्यर्थः -- इति
मोक्षयिष्यामि सर्वपापेभ्यस्त्वं? मा शुचः इति चोपक्रमोपसंहारौ न स्याताम्
एवं वर्तमानं त्वां मत्प्राप्तिविरोधिभ्यः अनादिकालसंचितानन्ताकृत्यकरणकृत्याकरणरूपेभ्यः सर्वेभ्यः पापेभ्यो मोक्षयिष्यामि
सर्वपापेभ्यस्त्वं? मा शुचः इति चोपक्रमोपसंहारौ न स्याताम्

advaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita.english_rendering: Abandon all dharmas — and by 'dharma' here adharma too is included, since the aim is naishkarmya (actionlessness): renounce all karma entirely. Then seek refuge in Me alone — the one Self (sarvātman), present equally in all beings, birthless and deathless — understanding that nothing other than Me exists. I shall liberate you from all sins, meaning from the bondage constituted by dharma and adharma alike, by illuminating your own Self-nature; as declared, 'I destroy ignorance by the shining lamp of jnana' (10.11). Grieve not.
advaita.divergence_note: Śaṅkara: 'māmekam sarvātmānam... aham tvā evam niścitabuddhim sarvapāpebhyah sarvadharmaadharmabandhanarupebhyah moksayisyami svātmabhāvaprakāśīkaranena' — liberation is by Self-revelation, not by karmic merit. The entire surrounding discussion establishes that jnāna alone is the means to moksha; karma cannot produce the eternal, and samuccaya (combination of jnāna+karma) is also refuted.

viśiṣṭādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
viśiṣṭādvaita.english_rendering: Performing karma-yoga, jnāna-yoga, and bhakti-yoga as they befit your adhikāra — but relinquishing the sense of doership and the attachment to fruits (phala-karma-kartrtva-parityāga) — surrender to Me alone as doer, as the one to be worshipped, as the goal, and as the means. This is the śāstriya-parityāga declared earlier: not abandoning action but abandoning agency. I shall liberate you from all sins — the beginningless accumulated mass of krtya-akarana and akrtya-karana — which are too vast for finite atonements (krcchracāndrāyana etc.) to exhaust. Grieve not.
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Rāmānuja: 'fala-karma-kartrtva-ādi-parityāgena parityajya mām ekam eva kartāram ārādhyam prāpyam upāyam ca anusamdhatsva.' The surrounding verses on sāttvika-tyāga (18.9, 18.11) establish that renunciation means renouncing fruit and agenthood, not action itself. Prapatti replaces finite prayascitta.

dvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
dvaita.english_rendering: Abandon the fruits of all dharmas — relinquish karma-phala — but continue the actions themselves, since warfare has already been commanded. 'Dharma-tyāga' here means phala-tyāga, as stated at 18.11: 'he who abandons the fruits of action is called a tyāgī.' Come to Me — Hari alone, who is absolutely independent (svatantra) — as your sole refuge. I shall free you from all sins. Do not grieve.
dvaita.divergence_note: Madhva (terse): 'dharmatyāgah phalatyāgah. kathamanyathā yuddhavidhih. yastukarmaphalatyāgī sa tyāgītyabhidhīyate [18.11] iti coktam.' The brevity is deliberate: Madhva has already established that Hari is the only independent reality; the verse's command collapses entirely to phala-tyāga + Hari-śaraṇa.

śuddhādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
śuddhādvaita.english_rendering: If you are unable even to perform the principal path (mukhya-karma) enjoined thus far, then here is the secondary provision (anukalpam): abandon all dharmas of the maryādā-mārga (the path of regulated observance) that obstruct entry into the puṣṭi-mārga (path of grace), and take exclusive refuge in Me. Krsna himself is the primary agent (mukhya-kartā); surrender means acknowledging His agency over all one's acts. I shall free you — by the very power of My descent (sarvanirodha-avatāratva) — from all sins, including the seemingly impossible (dussādhya) sins of killing kin. Grieve not.
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: Vallabha: 'muyakhyakartari paradavatāyām paramātmani dharmakarmabhāram sannyasya madekasaranatayā madukta-kāritvam ucitam.' He distinguishes: 'sarvadharmān' = dharmas of the maryādā-mārga that obstruct puṣṭi entry; abandoning them is not antinomian but entry into Krsna's own kāritra (activity). 'Moksayisyāmi' (causative) rather than 'mocayisyāmi' signals Krsna's irreversible taking-on of the burden.

bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
bhakti.english_rendering: Now the most secret of secrets is declared: with firm conviction that through My bhakti alone everything shall be accomplished, abandon the burden of rule-bound compliance (vidhikaikarya) and become exclusively devoted to Me as your one refuge. Do not grieve fearing that sin may accrue from abandoning prescribed karma. Since you are one who has taken Me alone as refuge, I shall liberate you from all sins. Bhakti itself is the prayascitta.
bhakti.divergence_note: Śrīdhara: 'madbhaktyaiva sarvam bhavisyatīti drdhaviśvāsena vidhikaikaryam tyaktvā madekasarano bhava. evamvartamānah karmatyāganimitam pāpam syādīti mā śucah.' The commentary is compact but decisive: the verse is the most secret (guhyatamam) because it replaces śāstric compliance with pure bhakti-śraddhā.

advaita-bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita-bhakti.english_rendering: Abandon all dharmas — not in the sense of literal renunciation (that would suit only sannyāsins, and Arjuna is a ksatriya), but by ceasing to regard dharma as independently efficacious. Seek refuge in Me alone — the Īśvara who is the very substrate (adhisthātar) and fruit-giver (phaladātar) of all dharma. Even while your duties continue, regard them with indifference (anādara) and attend solely to Bhagavān Vāsudeva in unbroken awareness (tailadhārāvad avicchinnayā). Since I am the agent behind all dharma, I shall free you — even without prayascitta — from the sins born of killing kinsmen. Grieve not.
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusūdana: 'dharmāh santu na santu vā kim tairanvasāpeksaih. bhagavadanugrahādeva tvanyanirapeksād... sarvadharmakarya-kāritvānmadeka-saranasya nāsti dharmāpeksā.' He explicitly refuses both the pure sannyāsa reading (Śaṅkara) and a literal karma-abandonment reading, arriving at bhagavad-ekaśaraṇatā as the synthesis available to all āśramas — brahmacarī, grhastha, vānaprastha, bhiksu alike.

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: The verse commands abandoning 'all dharmas' — but the Gītā has spent 17 chapters insisting on dharma. Is this a contradiction, a culmination, or a different register of address entirely?

Each school reads 'śaraṇam vraja' differently: as jnāna-niṣṭhā (Śaṅkara), as prapatti-upāya (Rāmānuja), as phala-tyāga + Hari-refuge (Madhva), as puṣṭi-entry (Vallabha), as bhakti-trust (Śrīdhara), as bhagavad-ekaśaraṇatā across āśramas (Madhusūdana). What does it mean that a single imperative (vraja) can carry all these meanings without collapsing?
Krsna says 'I will liberate you from all sins' (moksayisyāmi). Does this promise apply universally, or only to those with adhikāra — and who decides?
The verse appears as the Gītā's own charama-śloka, its final teaching. Why does the text end not with a philosophical thesis but with a command and a promise?

Madhusūdana reads the verse as available equally to all āśramas (brahmacarī through bhiksu). Madhva limits grace to the yogya jīva. What is at stake — practically and soteriologically — in that difference?

If 'sarva-dharmān parityajya' means 'abandon the sense of doership in dharma' (Rāmānuja, Madhusūdana) rather than 'abandon all practice,' how does one enact that abandonment in a life structured by obligations?

The verse promises freedom from 'all sins' (sarva-pāpebhyo). Vallabha includes the seemingly impossible sin of killing kin among these. What is the theological structure of a promise that overrides ordinary karmic logic?

everyday_applications.advaita: When paralyzed by the weight of your accumulated roles — parent, professional, citizen — recognize that the burden arises from identifying the Self with the agent. Lay down the roles not by abandoning your work but by ceasing to treat the doer as real. The anxiety dissolves not through more doing but through inquiry: who is it that fears the sin?
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: In a high-stakes decision where no atonement seems adequate for past failures, stop calculating what penance will balance the ledger. Surrender the calculation itself — the anxiety about merit — to Bhagavān as the one who holds the account. Then act from your capacity, trusting that the shortfall is His to cover.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Each morning, complete your assigned work fully and well — do not short-change the task. But at day's end, deposit the outcome entirely with Hari: neither claim credit for success nor carry forward the weight of failure. The deposit is not resignation; it is the recognition that Hari alone is independent.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: When you find yourself performing religious or ethical practices as a kind of barter — doing good to earn grace — notice the exhaustion in that arithmetic. Vallabha's reading: drop the commerce. Let Krsna's own delight (ānanda) be the reason you act. The gopikās had no formal practice; their exclusive attention to Krsna was itself the whole path.
everyday_applications.bhakti: When anxiety about doing the right thing correctly — following every rule — crowds out the devotion itself, Śrīdhara's reading applies: the trust that bhakti alone will take care of it is the point. Perform your duties, but hold them lightly, the way a child holds a parent's hand — not gripping, just present.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana offers this for the person who cannot renounce and cannot fully surrender: simply turn toward Vāsudeva continuously, like oil flowing without break, even while your duties run. You need not resolve the tension between your obligations and your longing for liberation. The unbroken turning is itself the resolution.
